html:
EPDM Systems for Mill Conversions and Established Roofs Across Spartanburg County

EPDM has a long track record on Upstate SC commercial buildings, and we still spec it where the roof geometry, budget, or existing system calls for it. A lot of the converted mill buildings around Startex, Arcadia, and Pacolet were reroofed with EPDM at some point in the past two or three decades, and matching or replacing that system correctly requires understanding how it was originally installed.

Attachment Options and When Each Makes Sense

Ballasted EPDM, held down with stone or paver ballast rather than fasteners or adhesive, works well on structurally sound roofs with enough dead load capacity, and it is often the lowest-cost option for a large flat roof. Mechanically attached EPDM uses fastening plates and bars along seams and perimeter, which suits roofs where added ballast weight is not an option. Fully adhered EPDM bonds the membrane directly to the substrate with adhesive, giving a cleaner, lower-profile result that some owners prefer on visible or occupied-roof buildings.

We evaluate structural capacity, existing deck condition, and the building's exposure before recommending which attachment method fits a specific project rather than defaulting to whichever is fastest to install.

Seam Splicing and Long-Term Performance

EPDM seams are spliced with either seam tape or liquid-applied adhesive rather than heat welded, which changes how we approach quality control during installation. Splice width, primer application, and roller pressure at the seam all affect long-term adhesion, and we document each of those steps for warranty purposes. Seams remain the most common source of EPDM leaks over a roof's life, so we treat that step as the one that determines whether a roof performs for fifteen years or five.

Black Membrane and Heat Considerations

Standard EPDM is black, which absorbs more solar heat than a white TPO or PVC membrane through Spartanburg's long, humid summer. That thermal load affects membrane expansion and contraction cycling over the year and can influence cooling costs for climate-controlled space below. White EPDM is available where an owner wants the reflectivity benefit without switching membrane types entirely, and we quote both options on request.

  • Structural and deck evaluation to confirm ballast, mechanical, or adhered attachment fits
  • Insulation and cover board layout matched to code and drainage needs
  • Membrane layout minimizing field seams where possible
  • Splice preparation, priming, and pressure-rolled seam bonding
  • Flashing at curbs, penetrations, and perimeter termination bars
  • Post-installation seam inspection before warranty sign-off

Working on Older Mill and Industrial Buildings

Buildings around Glendale, Saxon, and Clifton that started as textile mills often have wood or aging steel decks, unusual drainage patterns, and structural quirks that were never intended for modern rooftop mechanical loads. EPDM's flexibility and lighter dead-load options make it a practical fit on some of these conversions, but the deck and structural evaluation has to come first. We do not spec a membrane system on a legacy building without confirming what the roof structure can actually carry, and on wood-deck conversions in particular, we check for rot or prior water damage that the previous roof may have been hiding rather than assuming the deck is sound because the building is still standing.

Repair Versus Full Replacement

EPDM roofs that are otherwise sound but showing isolated seam failures or flashing deterioration are often better candidates for targeted repair than full tear-off, particularly on ballasted systems where disturbing the whole roof means moving a significant amount of stone. We assess remaining service life honestly before recommending a full replacement over a repair, since a premature tear-off is a cost the owner did not need to carry.

Documentation and Warranty Registration

EPDM warranties from major manufacturers cover material defects and, on many projects, workmanship for a defined term, but coverage terms differ meaningfully between ballasted, mechanically attached, and fully adhered installations. We register the completed system with the manufacturer and provide the resulting warranty documentation to the owner, along with a summary of what is and is not covered, since that distinction matters more at claim time than at installation time.

We also keep a record of splice locations, ballast weight, and fastener pattern for each project, which speeds up future repair calls because the crew arriving for a service visit already knows how the roof was built rather than starting from scratch. That record also helps if a portion of the roof needs to be opened for a future mechanical retrofit, since the installing crew can see exactly where seams and fasteners fall before cutting into the membrane.

Common Questions About EPDM Roofing

Is EPDM still a good option for new construction, or is it mainly for existing roofs?

We still spec it on new work where budget or roof geometry favors it, though TPO has become more common for new low-slope construction in this market.

Can you match an existing ballasted EPDM system on an addition or adjacent building?

In most cases yes, provided the structural capacity supports the ballast load, which we confirm before quoting the match.

How do you handle EPDM seam repairs on an older mill-building roof?

We inspect the existing splice condition, clean and reprime the seam area, and reseal using compatible tape or adhesive rather than assuming a generic patch will bond correctly.

Does white EPDM cost more than standard black membrane?

Typically yes, by a modest margin. We quote both so owners can weigh the reflectivity benefit against the added cost for their specific building.

How long does a properly installed EPDM roof typically last in this climate?

Service life depends heavily on seam quality and maintenance, but well-installed and maintained EPDM regularly reaches two decades or more of service in the Upstate.
